titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Video Verité papers
titlestmt.author: by Elise Riley

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Tuesday April 2, 2024 at 03:02

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: Correspondence, research files, notes production files, photographs, financial papers, legal papers, electronic files, printed material, and other papers by or relating to the documentary production company, Video Verité.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Box 42 (financial and student records): Restricted until 2037.
Box 121 (computer media): Restricted fragile. Access copies of digital files may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 123 (audiovisual materials): Restricted fragile. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased on the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund and the Alfred Z. Baker, Jr. Fund, 2022.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This collection is arranged in five series: I. Biographical materials, 1972-2012. II. Early works, 1965-2020. III. Documentary files, 1895-2022. IV. Audiovisual materials, undated. V. Computer media, undated.

bioghist.head: Video Verité/ Alan and Susan Raymond
bioghist.p: Video Verité is a film production company owned and operated by Alan and Susan Raymond. Together, the Raymonds have produced over 20 documentary films focusing on social issues of the past five decades. The Raymonds are award winning producers/directors and have won a Pioneer Award from The International Documentary Association, an Academy Award for Best Feature Documentary, 11 Emmy Awards, and were selected as Emmy Legends in 2011 for their work.

Alan Raymond (1943 - ) graduated from New York University in 1965 with a Bachelor of Arts in Film Studies. While a film student, he worked as an assistant film editor for Drew Associates from 1965-1967. He moved to Chicago to work for The Film Group, a small production company, where he met Susan.

Susan Raymond nee Cullinan (1945 - ) graduated from Depaul University in 1967 with a Bachelor of Arts in Sociology/Anthropology. To finance her education, she worked as a paralegal at a large law firm and later at a Daughters of Charity settlement house. She met Alan while he worked at The Film Group where her brother-in-law, Howard Alk, worked.

Alan and Susan moved to New York City together, got married, and began working in the film production industry. The first project they both worked on was A Private Decision: The 1968 Presidential Election where Susan worked as a grip and then did sound, and Alan worked photography and production.

Early in their careers, the Raymonds worked for the New York PBS station WNET/13, Quest productions with Bill Jersey, and N.E.T. Playhouse with The Second City. They met Craig Gilbert, the future producer of An American Family (1973), working on the film, The Triumph of Christy Brown . Afterwards, Gilbert approached Alan and Susan to work film and sound for a project that eventually became An American Family . The Raymonds spent seven months in Santa Barbara filming the Loud family. They had major control of daily filming for the series and developed their cinema verité filming style.

Following the success and acclaim of An American Family , Alan and Susan Raymond formed Video Verité and began to produce their own documentary films. Their next project, Police Tapes (1977), saw them using low-light equipment to capture the nightly routines of New York City police officers on their patrols in the Bronx. The film won several awards including an Emmy Awards for Directing and Photography and Editing, an Alfred I. DuPont Award for Excellence in Journalism, and a George Foster Peabody Award.

The Raymonds went on to produce more documentary films based on social issues both domestic and international, such as the American incarceration system, inner-city education, music history, mental illness, and the effects of armed conflicts and war. They became well known for investing large amounts of time with their subjects to produce deeper narratives on screen. The Raymonds' documentaries include To Die for Ireland (1980), Doing Time: Life Inside the Big House (1990), I Am A Promise: The Children of Stanton Elementary School (1994), Children In War (2000), and How Do You Spell Murder (2003).

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Video Verité Papers. General Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.

relatedmaterial.head: Related Materials
relatedmaterial.p: Related materials include the Lance Loud and Loud Family papers (GEN MSS 1320).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Video Verité papers (1895-2022) include correspondence, production notes, film logs, transcripts, contracts, budgets, notebooks, research, photographs, negatives, awards, and press created by or relating to the film production company, Video Verite, and its owners, Alan and Susan Raymond.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series maintains order found at time of acquisition.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains interview transcripts, biographies, awards, and press related to Alan and Susan Raymond's professional careers as film producers and directors.

did.unittitle: Transcripts from Paley Center - #1 with Craig Gilbert and #2 with Mark Burnett

Certificate of Nomination for Award - Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Science
Robert F. Kennedy Journalism Award for Alan Raymond
Robert F. Kennedy Journalism Award for Susan Raymond

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged chronologically by date of creation.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains materials related to films Alan and Susan worked on prior to An American Family (1973) and the creation of their company, Video Verité in the early 1970s.

did.unittitle: How Do You Like the Bowery? correspondence and features
The Triumph of Christy Brown correspondence and background materials
A Private Decision: The 1968 Presidential Election production stills
A Private Decision: The 1968 Presidential Election negatives
A Selection of Fifty Works from Robert C. Scull
America's Pop Collector Contemporary Art at Auction press and background materials

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: This series is arranged into twenty-two sub-series chronologically by date of documentary's production.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series contains materials related to documentary films produced by Alan and Susan Raymond.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: An American Family (1973)
bioghist.p: An American Family (1973) chronicles the daily life of the Louds -- a white, upper-middle class family in Santa Barbara, California. This television documentary series is known as the birth of reality television. In twelve one-hour unscripted episodes, the show captures the separation and divorce of the parents, Bill and Pat Loud. It also documents the daily lives of their five children. The eldest, Lance Loud, was the first non-fictional openly gay person to recur on a television program. The Raymonds produced three additional projects featuring the Louds: American Family Revisted (1983), Lance Loud!: A Death in the American Family (2003), and An American Family Anniversary Edition (2011).

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains proposals, releases, photographs, correspondence, press, and reviews related to the television series, An American Family (1973).

did.unittitle: "Silence of Lance and Pat Loud" book excerpts and correspondence
WNET 25 year anniversary retrospective and press

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: The Police Tapes (1977)
bioghist.p: The Police Tapes (1977) documents the daily life of the 44th police precinct in the South Bronx during 1976. Using a small Sony Portapak black and white video camera and deck, Alan and Susan Raymond capture the dangers and frustrations of the officers. The documentary was a project at the TV Lab at the New York PBS station, WNET/13. The original 90-minute version only aired locally. The film received a Peabody Award, a Columbia School of Journalism DuPont Award, and a local New York Area Emmy Award for Outstanding Documentary. ABC News approached the Raymonds to air a shortened version for ABC News Closeup, which was the first time a network news organization broadcast an independently produced documentary. The Closeup version won a national Emmy Award for Outstanding Documentary and Editing. The Police Tapes is in the permanent collection of the Museum of Modern Art and the Bibliotheque Nationale.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains proposals, releases, photographs, correspondence, fan mail, press, and reviews related to the documentary film, The Police Tapes (1977).

did.unittitle: ABC publicity and trends in audience mail

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: Bad Boys (1978)
bioghist.p: Bad Boys (1978) is a two-hour documentary about the juvenile incarceration system and how American society treats those who enter the system. Alan and Susan Raymond focused on three public institutions in New York State: Bryant High School in Long Island City, Spofford Juvenile Center in New York City, and Brookwood Secure Center for Youth in Claverack, New York. While at Brookwood, the Raymonds interviewed 15-year-old Willie Bosket Jr., who upon release would commit a double homicide. Bosket's case directly led to the creation of New York's Juvenile Offender Act which allowed minors to be tried as adults. Bad Boys shares the same film style as The Police Tapes , both were filmed using a Sony Portapak camera and deck. After airing on PBS, the Raymonds were approached by CBS to adapt the documentary into a dramatic movie, but the project was never produced.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains proposals, scripts, transcripts, photographs, correspondence, editing notes, press, and reviews related to the documentary film, Bad Boys (1978).

did.unittitle: Library of Congress Copyright Office correspondence
National News Council reports, correspondence, and press
New York Times symposium biography and transcripts
"Cycle of Violence" story treatment by Henry Denker
"Legacy of Rage" teleplay by Henry Denker
Research: "All God's Children" by Fox Butterfield
Bad Boys script by Mark Medoff
Bad Boys revised draft by Mark Medoff
Bad Boys script by Mark Medoff

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series are arranged alphabetically by folder title.

bioghist.head: To Die for Ireland (1980)
bioghist.p: To Die for Ireland (1980) is a one-hour documentary made for ABC News Closeup. After The Police Tapes (1977) was broadcast on ABC, Alan and Susan Raymond became producer-directors for the documentary unit at ABC News. This film captures the relationship between the British Army and the Provisional IRA at the height of the Troubles in Northern Ireland. To Die for Ireland received a News and Documentary Emmy Award for Best Cinematography and was nominated for an Emmy Award for Directing and Outstanding Documentary.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains proposals, contracts, transcripts, photographs, correspondence, notes, press, and reviews related to the documentary film, To Die for Ireland (1980).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: The Third Coast (1981)
bioghist.p: The Third Coast (1981) is a one-hour documentary about Houston, Texas and the city's emergence as the third most powerful urban area in the US. The film aired nationally on PBS and captures vignettes of the city including the premiere of Karl Lagerfeld's collection at Neiman Marcus, a large singles apartment complex, an interview with oil tycoon Glenn McCarthy, the Lyndon Johnson Space Center, and more.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains proposals, photographs, correspondence, editing notes, press, research, and reviews related to the documentary film, The Third Coast (1981).

did.unittitle: Proposal budget, camera log, and music

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: Hooray for Hollywood (1981)
bioghist.p: Hooray for Hollywood (1981) is a one-hour documentary for ABC News Closeup which examines the relationship between art and commerce in Hollywood in 1980. It includes interviews with Ridley Scott on the set as he directs Blade Runner , Paul Schrader directing Cat People , Martin Scorsese directing King of Comedy , and Herbert Ross directing Pennies From Heaven , as well as interviews with Harrison Ford, Steve Martin, Cary Grant, Raquel Welch, Richard Rush, Peter O'Toole, Gordon Willis, Frank Yablans and others. This film was Nominated for a News & Documentary Emmy Award for Editing.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains transcripts, photographs, correspondence, editing notes, press, and reviews related to the documentary film, Hooray for Hollywood (1981).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: American Family Revisited (1983)
bioghist.p: American Family Revisited (1983) is a one-hour HBO Documentary Films program about the Loud family, reality television stars from the television series An American Family (1973). Ten years after the original series aired, Loud family members reflect on their experiences during filming and changes to their lives over the past decade. The documentary also captures the unique relationship the family developed with Alan and Susan Raymond.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains budgets, releases, transcripts, photographs, correspondence, editing notes, press, and reviews related to the documentary film, Amercan Family Revisted (1983).

did.unittitle: Video Verité / Loud Family correspondence and agreements

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: Elvis '56 (1987)
bioghist.p: Elvis '56 (1987) is a compilation documentary bringing together clips, audio interviews, and candid photographs of Elvis Presley from his breakthrough year in 1956. This HBO Documentary Films program is narrated by Levon Helm and features Elvis's early television performances including The Dorsey Brothers Show, Milton Berle, Steve Allen, and The Ed Sullivan Show. At the time of the documentary's release, Rolling Stone called Elvis '56 "the best documentary ever made on Elvis Presley."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains licenses, transcripts, research, correspondence, editing notes, press, and reviews related to the documentary film, Elvis '56 (1987).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: Into Madness (1988)
bioghist.p: Into Madness (1988) is a one-hour HBO Documentary Film program which follows three families with adult children diagnosed with schizophrenia. The families share personal stories, and the film explores different mental health care options. Into Madness received awards from the American Psychiatric Association and the National Alliance for the Mentally Ill.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains contracts, transcripts, photographs, correspondence, editing notes, press, research, and reviews related to the documentary film, Into Madness (1988).

did.unittitle: Research - Surviving Schizophrenia by E. Fuller Torrey

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: Police Chiefs (1989)
bioghist.p: Police Chiefs (1989) is a one-hour PBS documentary which profiles the police chiefs of three major cities in the US: Los Angeles Chief Darryl Gates, Houston Chief Lee Brown, and Minneapolis Chief Anthony Bouza. Bouza previously appeared in The Police Tapes when he was the Bronx Borough Commander. The film shows the police chiefs, their officers, and crime scenes.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains contracts, research, photographs, editing notes, press, and reviews related to the documentary film, Police Chiefs (1989).

did.unittitle: CPB [Corporation for Public Broadcasting] financial reports
Research - Chief: My Life in the LAPD by Daryl F. Gates
Doing Time: Life Inside The Big House (1990)

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: Doing Time: Life Inside The Big House (1990)
bioghist.p: Doing Time: Life Inside The Big House (1990) is a one-hour HBO America Undercover documentary about life inside the Lewisberg Federal Penitentiary in Lewisburg, Pennsylvania. Alan and Susan Raymond spent two months filming, and this marks the first time that the Federal Bureau of Prisons allowed documentary filmmakers into a penitentiary to film without restrictions. The film explores the psychological effects of long-term incarceration and the perceived futility of rehabilitation and parole. Doing Time: Life Inside The Big House received an Academy Award nomination for Best Documentary Feature and a News and Documentary Emmy Award for Outstanding Directing of a Documentary.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains proposals, scripts, transcripts, photographs, correspondence, editing notes, press, research, and reviews related to the documentary film, Doing Time: Life Inside The Big House (1990).

did.unittitle: HBO [Home Box Office] exclusive contract deal

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: Sweet Home Chicago (1991)
bioghist.p: Sweet Home Chicago (1991) is a one-hour BBC documentary about the history of the blues. Focusing on Chess Records label of Chicago, this documentary features interviews from James Cotton, Buddy Guy, John Lee Hooker, Mick Jagger, Johnnie Johnson, Koko Taylor, Junior Wells, Hubert Sumlin, and Phil and Marshall Chess. Also included is archival footage of performances by Muddy Waters, The Rolling Stones, Buddy Guy, Otis Spann, Willie Dixon, Sonny Boy Williamson, and Chuck Berry.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains contracts, transcripts, photographs, correspondence, editing notes, press, research, and reviews related to the documentary film, Sweet Home Chicago (1991).

did.unittitle: Collis, John - Story of Chess Records
I Am A Promise: The Children of Stanton Elementary School (1993)

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: I Am A Promise: The Children of Stanton Elementary School (1993)
bioghist.p: I Am A Promise: The Children of Stanton Elementary School (1993) is a 90-minute HBO Documentary Films program about the children of M. Hall Stanton Elementary School in North Philadelphia. At the time of filming, 90% of students at Stanton lived below the poverty line and came from single parent homes. Alan and Susan Raymond spent one year filming without restrictions. The narrative of the film follows the principal, Deanna Burney, as she handles a school that is under-funded, understaffed, and located in a crime filled neighborhood. I Am A Promise: The Children of Stanton Elementary School received an Academy Award for Best Documentary Feature, a Prime Time Emmy Award for Best Documentary, a Peabody Award, an Alfred I. DuPont Award, and a Robert F. Kennedy Award.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains proposals, scripts, transcripts, photographs, correspondence, editing notes, press, research, and reviews related to the documentary film, I Am A Promise: The Children of Stanton Elementary School (1993).

did.unittitle: HBO school documentary legal and insurance
Police Tapes and Doing Time foreign television sales
Into Madness, Doing Time, Police Tapes, I Am A Promise foreign television sales
National Cable Television Association 1994 awards calendar

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: Children in War (2001)
bioghist.p: Children in War (2001) is a 108-minute HBO Documentary Films program that examines the effects of war and terrorism on children. Alan and Susan Raymond filmed on location in Bosnia, Rwanda, Israel, Palestine, and Northern Ireland. The documentary shows war-torn neighborhoods, orphanages, refugee camps, and schools, and the narrative is told through the children's own words. Children in War (2001)received a Prime Time Emmy Award for Best Documentary, a UNESCO Film Award. Susan Raymond was nominated for a Prime Time Emmy Award for her narration and she and Alan published a companion book also titled Children in War .

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains proposals, scripts, transcripts, artwork, photographs, correspondence, editing notes, press, research, and reviews related to the documentary film, Children in War (2001).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: How Do You Spell Murder? (2003)
bioghist.p: How Do You Spell Murder? (2003) is a 40-minute HBO Documentary film about an inmate-run literacy program at the New Jersey State Prison in Trenton. Alan and Susan Raymond spent a year at the maximum-security prison filming. At the time the average sentence was 50 years and 75% of the inmates were illiterate. How Do You Spell Murder? (2003) received the Literacy In America Award for Best Documentary of the Year.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains proposals, artwork, photographs, correspondence, editing notes, press, research, and reviews related to the documentary film, How Do You Spell Murder? (2003).

did.unittitle: HBO's screening of How Do You Spell Murder? Photography by Stephen Lonekin/Film magic
Lance Loud!: A Death in An American Family (2003)

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: Lance Loud!: A Death in an American Family (2003)
bioghist.p: Lance Loud!: A Death in An American Family (2003) is a one-hour PBS documentary following Lance Loud, eldest son of the Loud Family from the documentary television series An American Family (1973), in the last days of his life. When he entered hospice diagnosed with HIV and Hepatitis C, Lance requested Alan and Susan Raymond return to film a final episode of An American Family and bring closure to the series. Lance reflects on being the first out, gay, non-fictionalized person to appear on television, being a Queer icon, his relationship with Andy Warhol, his time in the punk band the Mumps, his writing career, and the long shadow An American Family (1973) cast on his life.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains budgets, scripts, transcripts, photographs, correspondence, editing notes, press, and reviews related to the documentary film, Lance Loud!: A Death in an American Family (2003).

did.unittitle: Mumps and The Am Fam Variations music
Lance Loud and Alan Raymond Pitch letters
Tate Modern / The Hepatitis C Trust
Mumps Band with Lance Loud, black and white pictures
Lance, Pat, and Michelle Loud in hospice
The "Oh for Crying Out Loud" Portfolio of Journalistic Brilliance

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: The Congregation (2004)
bioghist.p: The Congregation (2004) is a two-hour PBS documentary that profiles a progressive United Methodist Church during a leadership transition. Alan and Susan Raymond spent over two years filming at the church in the Germantown neighborhood of Philadelphia. Conflict starts when the more traditional Fred Day, newly appointed minister, arrives replacing the recently retired progressive pastor after 37 years of service. The congregation begins to clash over Reverend Day's style and their associate pastor, Reverend Beth Stroud, publicly comes out as gay that leads to a trial that defrocks her during the filming.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains contracts, budgets, transcripts, photographs, correspondence, editing notes, press, research, and reviews related to the documentary film, The Congregation (2004).

did.unittitle: Eastern Pennsylvania Conference: The United Methodist Church
Open the Doors, See All the People
This week at FUMCOG [First United Methodist Church of Germantown]

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: Hard Times at Douglass High (2008)
bioghist.p: Hard Times at Douglass High (2008) is a two-hour HBO Documentary Films program about student life at Frederick Douglass High School in West Baltimore, MD. The Raymonds spent a full school year filming and examining how the staff work to meet the demands of the No Child Left Behind Act in a school with low test scores, teacher shortages, and a 50% drop out rate.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains budgets, transcripts, photographs, editing notes, press, research, and reviews related to the documentary film, Hard Times at Douglass High (2008).

did.unittitle: Publicity photos produced by Alan and Susan Raymond
Press release filmmaker question and answer fact sheet

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: Journey into Dyslexia (2011)
bioghist.p: Journey Into Dyslexia (2011) is a 76-minute HBO Documentary Films program about dyslexic children and adults and their experiences in school and life. Interviewees express frustration about being misunderstood by teachers and the emotional impact dyslexia has had on their lives. Stories are told by students from 2nd grade to college, Steve Walk, Ben Foss, Jonathan Mooney, Erin Brockovich, and Dr. Coral Greider. Journey Into Dyslexia received an International Dyslexia Association Award for Best Documentary.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains contracts, transcripts, photographs, editing notes, press, research, and reviews related to the documentary film, Journey into Dyslexia (2011).

did.unittitle: Wigan, Willard - I Spy with My Little Eye

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: An American Family Anniversary Edition (2011)
bioghist.p: An American Family Anniversary Edition (2011) is a PBS two-hour feature film version of the original 1973 twelve-hour television series An American Family . Alan and Susan Raymond produced and reedited this version to resolve music clearances that prevented the original series from being sold or exhibited commercially.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains proposals, transcripts, press, and reviews related to the documentary film, An American Family Anniversary Edition (2011).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: Cinema Verite (2011)
bioghist.p: Cinema Verite (2011) is an HBO dramatic film about the making of the PBS series, An American Family . Directed by Shari Springer Berman and Robert Pulcini, the movie stars Diane Lane as Pat Loud, Tim Robbins as Bill Loud, James Gandolfini as Craig Gilbert, Patrick Fugit as Alan Raymond, and Shanna Collins as Susan Raymond. Alan and Susan Raymond served as consultants and worked with the writers, directors, actors, and production designers for two years to make the film.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains transcripts, photographs, correspondence, press, and reviews related to the dramatic film, Cinema Verite (2011).

did.unittitle: Toe Tag Parole: To Live and Die on Yard A (2015)

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Materials in this sub-series maintain order found at time of acquisition.

bioghist.head: Toe Tag Parole: To Live and Die on Yard A (2015)
bioghist.p: Toe Tag Parole: To Live and Die on Yard A (2015) is an 80-minute HBO documentary about an experimental yard at a maximum-security California State Prison. Yard A is for men serving sentences of life without parole that is free of violence, drug use, and racial tension. The documentary explores the ethics of a life without the possibility of parole sentence and rehabilitation for those convicted of capital crimes. Three men featured in the film submitted the documentary to the California Parole Board and had their sentences commuted by the Governor of California.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This sub-series contains transcripts, photographs, correspondence, editing notes, press, research, and reviews related to the documentary film, Toe Tag Parole: To Live and Die on Yard A (2015).

did.unittitle: Ken Hartman photos from Polaroid originals
LWOP [Life Without Parole] R&D Reel for HBO
When I Die, They'll Send Me Home
Williams, Dortell - Looking in on Lockdown
Mumps: Crocodile Tears/ Waiting For The World To Catch Up
